blocks.text: Now a man from the house of Levi went and married a Levite woman.
1 : Moses’s parents, both from the priestly tribe of , are named in 6.20. , Moses’s mother, the third in a series of twelve women featured in chs 1–2; see 1.15n.
The woman conceived and bore a son; and when she saw that he was a fine baby, she hid him three months.
2 : , lit., “saw that he was good,” echoes the language of creation in Gen 1.
When she could hide him no longer she got a papyrus basket for him, and plastered it with bitumen and pitch; she put the child in it and placed it among the reeds on the bank of the river.

3 : The Heb word for appears elsewhere in the Bible only as a designation for Noah’s ark (Gen 6.14), on which Noah and his family and the animals are saved. Here it is the means for rescuing the person who will save the Israelites. , see 13.18n.
His sister stood at a distance, to see what would happen to him.

4 : Moses’s , named Miriam in 15.20 (perhaps in a different source), is the fourth woman of the story (see 1.15n.). She appears in more books of the Hebrew Bible (Numbers, Deuteronomy, 1 Chronicles, and Micah) than any other woman.
The daughter of Pharaoh came down to bathe at the river, while her attendants walked beside the river. She saw the basket among the reeds and sent her maid to bring it.
5 : The is the fifth woman of chs 1–2; see 1.15n.
When she opened it, she saw the child. He was crying, and she took pity on him. “This must be one of the Hebrews’ children,” she said.
Then his sister said to Pharaoh’s daughter, “Shall I go and get you a nurse from the Hebrew women to nurse the child for you?”
7 : Hiring a wet was usually a practice of elites in the ancient Near East.
Pharaoh’s daughter said to her, “Yes.” So the girl went and called the child’s mother.
Pharaoh’s daughter said to her, “Take this child and nurse it for me, and I will give you your wages.” So the woman took the child and nursed it.
When the child grew up, she brought him to Pharaoh’s daughter, and she took him as her son. She named him Moses, “because,” she said, “I drew him out of the water.”
1–10 : As in the birth legends of other heroic figures in ancient literature, the miraculous rescue of the doomed infant Moses signifies that he is destined for greatness.

10 : , an Egyptian name meaning “is born” and often joined with a god’s name in Egyptian personal names (e.g., Thutmoses, Ahmoses, Rameses), is given a Heb etymology (“he who draws out”) in anticipation of Moses’s role in drawing his people through the sea (14.21–29; cf. Isa 63.11).
One day, after Moses had grown up, he went out to his people and saw their forced labor. He saw an Egyptian beating a Hebrew, one of his kinsfolk.
11 : , he identifies with his people even though he was raised in the Egyptian court.
He looked this way and that, and seeing no one he killed the Egyptian and hid him in the sand.
When he went out the next day, he saw two Hebrews fighting; and he said to the one who was in the wrong, “Why do you strike your fellow Hebrew?”
He answered, “Who made you a ruler and judge over us? Do you mean to kill me as you killed the Egyptian?” Then Moses was afraid and thought, “Surely the thing is known.”
14 : , Heb “sar”; cf. 18.21,25. , anticipates one of Moses’s many roles; see 18.13–27n.
When Pharaoh heard of it, he sought to kill Moses. But Moses fled from Pharaoh. He settled in the land of Midian, and sat down by a well.
11–15 : In the first two episodes of his adult life, Moses saves one Hebrew and tries to adjudicate between two others; both roles will recur and involve all his people.
14–15a : Moses is rejected by his own people, and Pharaoh seeks his life.

15b : , in northwest Arabia. The Midianites, said to be descendants of Abraham and Keturah (Gen 25.2), were caravaneers whose routes stretched across Sinai toward Egypt. A , where Moses meets his future wife, a pattern appearing in the stories of Rebekah (Gen 24) and Rachel (Gen 29).
The priest of Midian had seven daughters. They came to draw water, and filled the troughs to water their father’s flock.
16 : Moses’s future father‐in‐law is a , who plays an important role in ch 18. , making a total of twelve female figures (see 1.15n.).
But some shepherds came and drove them away. Moses got up and came to their defense and watered their flock.

17 : Saved by the daughter of a king, Moses now saves the daughters of a priest, one of whom will save him (4.24–26); the motif of saving recurs, anticipating the ultimate salvation or deliverance at the sea (13.17–15.21).
When they returned to their father Reuel, he said, “How is it that you have come back so soon today?”
18 : , elsewhere called Jethro (as 3.1) or Hobab (Judg 4.11), likely reflecting different ancient sources.
They said, “An Egyptian helped us against the shepherds; he even drew water for us and watered the flock.”
19 : Probably because of his clothing, Moses is mistaken for an .
He said to his daughters, “Where is he? Why did you leave the man? Invite him to break bread.”
Moses agreed to stay with the man, and he gave Moses his daughter Zipporah in marriage.
21 : , lit., “gave … as a wife,” a Heb idiom for marriage.
She bore a son, and he named him Gershom; for he said, “I have been an alien residing in a foreign land.”
15b–22 : Moses flees to Midian and marries.
After a long time the king of Egypt died. The Israelites groaned under their slavery, and cried out. Out of the slavery their cry for help rose up to God.
23 : They , the Egyptians eventually will cry louder (11.6), and the Israelites will cry again at the sea (14.10). Their , that is, “their work.”
God heard their groaning, and God remembered his covenant with Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob.
24 : Referring to the ancestral (Heb “berit,” as in 6.4–5; cf. 25.16n.) links Exodus with Genesis and anticipates the Sinai covenant (Ex 19–24).
God looked upon the Israelites, and God took notice of them.

1.1–15.21: Israel in and out of Egypt. The Israelites are oppressed in Egypt; but they escape through the intervention of their God, whose identity is revealed to their heroic leader Moses, who then carries out God’s directives to secure their release.
2.1–25: The emergence of Moses.

23–25 : A postscript to the marriage episode provides a transition to the account of Moses becoming deliverer of his people in response to God’s call. is mentioned in the midwives episode but only now is said to be aware of the Israelites’ plight.
